title: New Estimates for Navier–Stokes and the Inviscid Limit Problem
speaker: Jincheng Yang
abstract:
In this talk, I will present several a priori interior and boundary trace estimates for the 3D incompressible Navier–Stokes equation, which recover and extend the current picture of higher derivative estimates in the mixed norm. Then we discuss the applications in the inviscid limit problem, with both characteristic and noncharacteristic boundary conditions. In particular, we provide estimates on layer separation and energy dissipation in the zero viscosity limit.
This talk will be based on several work in collaboration with Alexis Vasseur, Vincent Martinez, and Anna Mazzucato.

proof_stages.label: Finite-energy weak and suitable Navier-Stokes framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes the three-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations and recalls Leray-Hopf and suitable weak solutions, their global and local energy inequalities, and the unresolved uniqueness problem in these classes.

proof_stages.label: Scaling diagram and known regularity thresholds
proof_stages.summary: Known velocity and velocity-gradient regularity criteria are placed in an Lp_t Lq_x scaling diagram, showing the gap between finite-energy a priori estimates and the critical spaces that imply smoothness and uniqueness.

proof_stages.label: Epsilon regularity and partial-regularity mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews the local epsilon-regularity criterion based on rescaled energy dissipation, explains the covering argument giving a one-dimensional singular set, and later invokes a one-scale version as the stopping criterion for derivative estimates.

proof_stages.label: Scale-function trace estimates for vorticity derivatives
proof_stages.summary: After passing to the vorticity equation, the new estimate controls every higher vorticity derivative by one solution-dependent scale function and bounds traces of that scale function on lower-dimensional sets in weak Lebesgue spaces.

proof_stages.label: Inviscid-limit formulation and classical boundary-layer background
proof_stages.summary: The inviscid-limit problem is formulated by comparing small-viscosity Navier-Stokes solutions with a fixed smooth Euler solution, with Prandtl boundary-layer theory and Kato's conditional criterion recalled as the classical context.

proof_stages.label: Relative-energy reduction to boundary friction
proof_stages.summary: A relative energy estimate identifies three central quantities—separation from Euler, viscous energy dissipation, and work by boundary stress—and shows that controlling the boundary-friction term controls the other two.

proof_stages.label: Uniform boundary-vorticity trace bound and inviscid-limit upper estimates
proof_stages.summary: A boundary analogue of the vorticity trace estimate, uniform under inviscid scaling, bounds the boundary-stress pairing and yields universal upper bounds of order A^3 T S for separation, dissipation, and boundary work.

proof_stages.label: Kato recovery and noncharacteristic boundary conditions
proof_stages.summary: The same method recovers Kato's criterion and extends the analysis to prescribed noncharacteristic boundary data, where matching upper and lower dissipation bounds show that Kato's vanishing-dissipation condition fails.
